event: Preceramic Archaic-period Amerindian hunter-gatherers (the earliest known inhabitants) settle Curacao, leaving behind shell middens and stone tools.
Caquetio people of Arawak (Caiquetio) origin, related to mainland Venezuelan groups, migrate to the island and establish farming and fishing communities.
Spanish explorer Alonso de Ojeda, accompanied by Amerigo Vespucci, becomes the first European to reach Curacao, claiming it for the Spanish Crown.
The Spanish deport much of the Caquetio population to Hispaniola as forced labor, declaring the islands 'islas inutiles' (useless islands) for lacking gold.
Spain re-establishes a settlement on Curacao, introducing cattle, horses, and goats and using the island chiefly as a ranching outpost.
The Dutch West India Company seizes Curacao from Spain, recognizing the value of its deep natural harbor (Schottegat) for trade and naval operations.
The Dutch begin fortifying Willemstad and developing the island as a strategic base in the Caribbean.
Peter Stuyvesant is appointed director of Curacao before later governing New Amsterdam (New York).
Jewish settlers, many of Sephardic origin from Amsterdam and Brazil, begin arriving and become a foundational community in Curacao's commerce.
Sephardic Jews establish a permanent community, later founding Mikve Israel-Emanuel, the oldest continuously used synagogue in the Americas.
The Dutch West India Company makes Curacao a major hub of the transatlantic slave trade, with Willemstad serving as a regional slave-trading depot.
Willemstad's harbor becomes a key entrepot where enslaved Africans are sold and traded across the Caribbean and Spanish Main.
Plantation agriculture and salt production expand, while the island's economy increasingly centers on trade and the re-export of goods and enslaved people.
A slave uprising on the plantations is suppressed by colonial authorities.
A major slave revolt led by Tula and Bastian Karpata erupts on the western plantations, demanding freedom before being violently crushed.
British forces briefly occupy Curacao during the Napoleonic Wars amid the collapse of Dutch authority in the Caribbean.
Britain again seizes Curacao, holding the island under British control during the wider European conflict.
Curacao is formally returned to the Netherlands under the Anglo-Dutch Treaty following the Napoleonic Wars.
The Netherlands abolishes slavery in its Caribbean colonies, emancipating the enslaved population of Curacao on July 1.
Curacao's distinctive orange-peel-based liqueur gains international fame as the island lends its name to the Curacao liqueur.
Royal Dutch Shell builds the Isla oil refinery to process Venezuelan crude, transforming Curacao's economy and drawing waves of migrant labor.
Venezuelan rebel leader Rafael Simon Urbina raids Willemstad, seizing weapons from Fort Amsterdam and taking the governor briefly hostage.
After the German invasion of the Netherlands, Allied (initially British, then American) troops are stationed on Curacao to guard its strategic refinery.
German U-boats attack tankers near Curacao and Aruba, targeting the vital oil supply that fueled the Allied war effort.
Universal suffrage is introduced, expanding political participation across the island's population.
Curacao becomes part of the Netherlands Antilles, an autonomous country within the Kingdom of the Netherlands under the Charter for the Kingdom.
Labor unrest at the oil refinery erupts into the Trinta di Mei riots, a turning point that advances Afro-Curacaoan political and cultural empowerment.
Papiamentu, the island's Creole language, gains growing official recognition in education and public life.
Royal Dutch Shell withdraws and sells the Isla refinery for a symbolic sum, leased to Venezuela's state oil company PDVSA, jolting the economy.
Aruba secedes from the Netherlands Antilles to gain separate 'status aparte,' leaving Curacao the dominant island in the federation.
The historic inner city and harbor of Willemstad are inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site for their well-preserved Dutch colonial architecture.
In a referendum, a majority of Curacao voters choose autonomous country status within the Kingdom of the Netherlands rather than full independence.
Curacao and the Netherlands sign agreements paving the way for the dissolution of the Netherlands Antilles.
On October 10 the Netherlands Antilles is dissolved and Curacao becomes a constituent country within the Kingdom of the Netherlands.
Politician Helmin Wiels, an outspoken anti-corruption advocate, is assassinated, sparking a national political crisis.
PDVSA halts operations at the Isla refinery amid Venezuela's economic collapse, deepening uncertainty over Curacao's industrial future.
An influx of Venezuelan migrants fleeing crisis strains Curacao's resources and immigration system.
The COVID-19 pandemic devastates Curacao's tourism-dependent economy, prompting Dutch financial aid packages tied to reform conditions.
Curacao agrees to a reform program with the Netherlands in exchange for liquidity support to recover from the pandemic-driven downturn.
Curacao continues efforts to diversify its economy toward tourism, financial services, and logistics while seeking a long-term solution for its idled refinery.
